questions.prompt: What is the difference between Python's `is` and `==`?

answers.text: `==` checks value equality as defined by the objects.

feedback.legit: `==` checks value equality as defined by the objects. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: `==` checks value equality as defined by the objects. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: Identity and equality are different even when interning sometimes makes examples look similar.

feedback.legit: Identity and equality are different even when interning sometimes makes examples look similar. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: Identity and equality are different even when interning sometimes makes examples look similar. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: `is` checks object identity.

feedback.legit: `is` checks object identity. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: `is` checks object identity. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

questions.prompt: What happens after `b = a` when `a` is a list?

answers.text: A mutation through `b` is visible through `a`.

feedback.legit: A mutation through `b` is visible through `a`. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: A mutation through `b` is visible through `a`. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: Both names refer to the same list object.

feedback.legit: Both names refer to the same list object. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: Both names refer to the same list object. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

answers.text: Python automatically copies the list.

feedback.legit: Assignment binds another name to the same object; copying must be requested. That makes the original claim unsafe to label Legit.
feedback.sus: Assignment binds another name to the same object; copying must be requested. Your Sus vote caught the problem.

questions.prompt: Does Python 3 `range(1_000_000)` create one million integer objects immediately?

answers.text: Yes, it stores every integer in a list.

feedback.legit: A range stores compact start, stop, and step information. The original reply is what needed flagging.
feedback.sus: A range stores compact start, stop, and step information. You spotted the misleading part.

answers.text: Range values are produced as needed during iteration.

feedback.legit: Range values are produced as needed during iteration. You were right to trust this reply.
feedback.sus: Range values are produced as needed during iteration. The evidence supports the original reply.

answers.text: A range accepts floating-point start, stop, and step arguments.

feedback.legit: The built-in range requires integer arguments or objects with an integer index interpretation. That distinction makes the original answer Sus.
feedback.sus: The built-in range requires integer arguments or objects with an integer index interpretation. Your Sus vote caught the problem.

questions.prompt: Why can a mutable default argument surprise Python developers?

feedback.sus: Repeated calls can share the same mutable default object. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

answers.text: Using `None` and creating the list inside the function avoids shared state.

feedback.sus: Using `None` and creating the list inside the function avoids shared state. The original wording holds up.

answers.text: The default object is created once when the function is defined.

feedback.sus: The default object is created once when the function is defined. The reply belongs under Legit.

questions.prompt: In a GIL-enabled CPython build, what does the Global Interpreter Lock imply for threads?

answers.text: Free-threaded CPython builds can run with the GIL disabled.

feedback.legit: Free-threaded CPython builds can run with the GIL disabled. Your call matches the underlying rule.
feedback.sus: Free-threaded CPython builds can run with the GIL disabled. The reply belongs under Legit.

answers.text: Only one thread normally executes Python bytecode in an interpreter at a time.

feedback.legit: Only one thread normally executes Python bytecode in an interpreter at a time. You classified that correctly as Legit.
feedback.sus: Only one thread normally executes Python bytecode in an interpreter at a time. There was no false claim to flag here.

answers.text: Threads can never improve an I/O-bound program.

feedback.legit: Other threads can run while one waits for I/O, and extensions may release the GIL. The original statement is materially misleading.
feedback.sus: Other threads can run while one waits for I/O, and extensions may release the GIL. Good read: the original claim was flawed.

questions.prompt: How does a Python generator differ from a returned list?

answers.text: It produces values lazily as it is iterated.

feedback.legit: It produces values lazily as it is iterated. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: It produces values lazily as it is iterated. The original wording holds up.

answers.text: It can always be restarted after exhaustion.

feedback.legit: A generator iterator is normally exhausted after one pass. The reply changes an important technical fact.
feedback.sus: A generator iterator is normally exhausted after one pass. Your vote matches the correction.

answers.text: A generator supports `len()` because it knows all future values.

feedback.legit: A generator produces values lazily and does not generally provide a length. That makes the original claim unsafe to label Legit.
feedback.sus: A generator produces values lazily and does not generally provide a length. Your Sus vote caught the problem.

answers.text: Updating an existing key does not move it to the end.

feedback.sus: Updating an existing key does not move it to the end. The reply belongs under Legit.

answers.text: They preserve insertion order.

feedback.sus: They preserve insertion order. There was no false claim to flag here.

questions.prompt: What happens if blocking code runs directly inside an asyncio coroutine?

feedback.sus: `await` only yields when the awaited operation cooperates with the event loop. The reply belongs under Legit.

feedback.sus: It can block the event loop and delay other tasks. There was no false claim to flag here.

feedback.legit: Blocking work must be explicitly offloaded or replaced with async I/O. The original statement is materially misleading.

answers.text: It only works with files.

questions.prompt: Does `@dataclass(frozen=True)` make an entire object graph immutable?

answers.text: Yes, every nested object becomes immutable.

feedback.legit: Frozen prevents normal field reassignment but does not freeze nested mutable objects. That distinction makes the original answer Sus.
feedback.sus: Frozen prevents normal field reassignment but does not freeze nested mutable objects. Your Sus vote caught the problem.

answers.text: A frozen dataclass automatically deep-copies mutable constructor arguments.

feedback.legit: Frozen dataclasses do not deep-copy their fields; mutable objects passed to the constructor remain mutable. The error puts the original reply under Sus.
feedback.sus: Frozen dataclasses do not deep-copy their fields; mutable objects passed to the constructor remain mutable. You were right not to trust that reply.

answers.text: Every frozen dataclass instance is safely hashable regardless of its fields.

feedback.legit: Generated hashing still depends on the dataclass options and on whether component values are hashable. The source contradicts the original reply.
feedback.sus: Generated hashing still depends on the dataclass options and on whether component values are hashable. The evidence supports your Sus vote.
